title: 9 Hidar — St. Samuel the Prophet & The Assembly
narrative:
Hidar

St. Samuel the Prophet (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Isaac of Nineveh (Departure)

St. Matrona of Perge, Monastic

The ninth day of Hidar places the Prophet Samuel beside the feast of the Departure of St. Isaac of Nineveh, and the convergence is one of the calendar's most theologically precise pairings. Samuel is the prophet who hears his name called in the night and who, when he finally understands who is calling, says the words that define the prophetic vocation for every subsequent generation: 'Speak, Lord, for your servant hears.' The prophetic posture — the disposition of total attentiveness that the interior life of Tiqimt spent an entire month cultivating — finds its apostolic expression in Hidar as the willingness to be spoken through, the vesting of the prophet as the instrument of the divine word. Samuel did not choose his vocation; he was chosen before birth and presented to the Temple and formed in the night watches, and the apostolic commission arrived before he knew what an apostolic commission was.

The Departure of St. Isaac of Nineveh is one of the most significant feast days of the entire calendar for the Ethiopian tradition — the death of the great mystic-theologian whose homilies have accompanied the community through four months of the liturgical year, monthly, and whose passing is honored as the completion of a life that was itself a liturgical act. Isaac died in his monastery after decades of writing and contemplation, and his death is described in the tradition with the quality of a natural completion — not the violence of the martyred or the drama of the visionary, but the gentle ending of a life that had been moving toward its source for so long that the final crossing was simply the last step of the journey already underway. In Hidar, the month of apostolic vesting, Isaac's departure is the testimony that the completion of the contemplative life is itself an apostolic act — that the monk who dies in his cell having given the Church its most thorough map of the interior journey has served the apostolic mission as fully as those who preached in the marketplaces.

St. Matrona of Perge was a fifth-century noblewoman of Thessalonica who, like several of the women of the desert tradition, dressed as a man and entered a monastery in Constantinople, where she lived for years as a monk named Babylas before her identity was discovered. She subsequently founded a community for women and lived to extreme old age, becoming one of the spiritual mothers of the Byzantine monastic tradition. Her story in Hidar's assembly is the testimony of the apostolic vesting across the boundaries that the world constructs between the possible and the impossible, the permitted and the restricted. Matrona found the form her vocation required by crossing the boundaries that her century assigned to her, and the vocation she fulfilled was real and lasting — her community survived her, and her witness travels in the calendar's memory.

Samuel, Isaac of Nineveh, and Matrona together on the ninth day present the apostolic vesting in three of its most searching forms: the night-call that forms the prophet before he understands what he is being formed for; the contemplative completion that is itself a form of mission; and the vocation pursued across every obstacle that convention erects against it. The month of Hidar is nine days in, and the community's vesting is deepening: the apostolic commission that the month is distributing is not a uniform garment cut to a standard size but a custom fitting — precisely matched to the particular life, the particular gifts, the particular obstacles of each person who stands before the divine Tailor.
